text.title: Testing in Go Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules every application of testing-go enforces. Each rule has a statement, a rationale anchored to industry practice, and a source citation.

rule.statement: Use table-driven tests with named struct fields for any function with multiple input/output pairs.
rule.rationale: Anonymous positional structs hide which field is which; named fields keep cases self-documenting as the table grows.

rule.statement: Wrap each row in `t.Run(tc.name, func(t *testing.T){...})` so a failing case names itself in output.
rule.rationale: Without `t.Run` the failure log says `TestFoo failed at row 5`; with `t.Run` it says `TestFoo/zero_price`.

rule.statement: Use `require.NoError` for must-have preconditions, `assert.Equal` for non-fatal field checks.
rule.rationale: `require` stops the test before a nil deref; `assert` lets multiple field mismatches surface in one run.

rule.statement: Mock collaborators via small interfaces (≤3 methods) defined in the consumer package.
rule.rationale: Large interfaces are hard to mock and signal the consumer is asking for too much. Defining at the consumer keeps the dependency direction correct.

rule.statement: Use `httptest.NewRecorder` for handler unit tests, `httptest.NewServer` for client tests.
rule.rationale: Recorder skips network; Server gives a real `http.Client` codepath. Mixing them masks bugs at the transport layer.

rule.statement: Prefer `b.Loop()` (Go 1.24+) over `for i := 0; i < b.N; i++` in new benchmarks.
rule.rationale: `b.Loop()` keeps the timer correct across setup/teardown and avoids `b.ResetTimer` boilerplate.

rule.statement: Separate integration tests with a `//go:build integration` build tag.
rule.rationale: Without a tag, slow DB/network tests run in the fast feedback loop and developers disable the lot.
